posts.text:
I think that within the city limits of a geographically small central city that contains a relatively small share of the metro area’s population, “medium scale residential neighborhood” should be the minimum designation — the suburbs exist!


Alex Taliadoros (@AlexCTaliadoros)

👋 DC housing policy Twitter 🏘️🏗️

The Office of Planning released their *draft* Future Land Use Map earlier today.

🗺️: dc2050.dc.gov/pages/draft-fu…

The map will be a big part of the city's next Comprehensive Plan rewri

Some of this designated “small scale residential” is *extremely* close to the central business district. 

The benefits to the city in terms of growth, jobs, and tax revenue of allowing big condos & apartments here is large.

My own neighborhood is designated for denser “medium scale” residential but we are surrounded by activity centers, rich in transit, and close to downtown.

Upzoning would grow the population, grow the tax base, and create a property value windfall for homeowners like me.

In a strange way, the politics of despair is downstream of UNDERRATING how many problems there are in the world and convincing yourself that only the most-discussed, least-tractable issues are worth caring about.

slowboring.com/p/worry-less-…

I kind of think Abughazaleh did very *well* when you consider her very weak actual ties to the district; she really did conjure a viable candidacy out of basically nothing.


Nate Silver (@NateSilver538)

There's plenty of appetite for progressivism, etc., within the Democratic electorate, but the Very Online version of it doesn't really travel well.





— https://nitter.net/NateSilver538/status/2034100554213773442#m

Is the state of the world really so uniquely bad that you can’t have children? I think parenting actually helps pull people out of unproductive doom spirals.

slowboring.com/p/worry-less-…

Something I try to talk through on today’s Politix:

— Oil futures are up way less than the fundamentals suggest; speculators believe the war will be short.

— Betting odds on senate Dems are up more than the polling, the opposite speculative bet.

politix.fm/p/mr-hollens-opus

The world is full of problems but getting yourself paralyzed with rage and despair doesn’t solve any of them 

slowboring.com/p/worry-less-…

The CBC professed to be furious that the governor of Illinois supported Illinois’ African-American lieutenant governor in a senate race over a different Black woman who the CBC happens to like because she’s a House member.
Melissa Bean cruising to victory shows that why Democratic primary voters want is Blue Dog retreads who used to be bank executives. I don’t make the rules, it’s a narrative.
Terrible outcome from a content standpoint

Strong affordability message carries the day again


Current Report (@Currentreport1)

BREAKING:

Kim Jong-un has won North Korea's parliamentary elections with 99.93% of the vote.

Video
  





— https://nitter.net/Currentreport1/status/2034002694755811570#m
We may have alienated our friends and provided Russia with a huge financial windfall, but at least we are propping up the stock market by giving away America’s biggest edge in AI.

This is the problem, from a national economic perspective, with coastal housing scarcity — the population growth should be happening in the places that are richer but instead the reverse is happening.


James Medlock (@jdcmedlock)

It's actually worse than I thought. Catch up growth assumes red states are growing faster on a per capita basis, but they're not. Hanania's charts were not per capita, so basically just measuring population growth. Red states are poorer, but they are also not convergi

A version of America that was building Mega City San Francisco Bay and had slower growth in Greater Phoenix etc would be a lot more prosperous on average.

slowboring.com/p/why-silicon…

Nobody is going to be able to rent a place to live if landlords aren't able to get rid of people who don't pay the rent.


7News DC (@7NewsDC)

Veronica Hegens thought she’d made a sound financial decision in September 2023 by renting out her row house in Southwest D.C.
Now the retired Army major is in danger of losing the property to foreclosure because of a tenant who hasn’t paid rent since October and who also has no immediate plans to leave.
bit.ly/4bfbEC6




— https://nitter.net/7NewsDC/st

This looks beautiful but literally all of human progress over time is reducing the share of the population that is doing subsistence farming.


Pamela (@PamelaBies)

Advice to the younger generation:
Skip the degree.
Buy land.
Become a farmer.





— https://nitter.net/PamelaBies/status/2033624903941861795#m

As Morris has documented extensively, the electoral benefits of moderation, though real, are also not enormous. 

The paper suggests that moderating SELECTIVELY — on affirmative action, merit pay for teachers, punishing non-violent crime, and using fossil fuels — would be much more potent.

As @gelliottmorris.com says, the research offers more nuanced advice than "just be more moderate." 

It says that moderation is beneficial on average, but that the impact of moderation varies issue-to-issue and that on a minority of issues moderation actually backfires.
I promise that I discussed this paper with the authors, that's why I had exclusive early access to write a New York Times op-ed about it.
I like that he's for school prayer AND for sex stores

The YIMBY movement has come to encompass a wide range of themes and ideas — single stair, missing middle, manufactured homes — and achieved national scope but the core question of growth (or lack thereof) in a handful of very high cost areas remains crucial. 

www.slowboring.com/p/why-silico...
In my defense, the text was wrapped up days ago
If I had a barrel of West Texas Intermediate crude oil laying around somewhere, I would not sell it for $100 given the current circumstances.
I think Caraballo is just restating the problem here not offering an alternative solution.

Today in the NYT, I discuss some fresh research on which moderations move the most voters.

www.nytimes.com/2026/03/16/o...
This is an amazing platform

If per person spending on the elderly goes up and the elderly share of the population also goes up, everything else gets squeezed and everyone is mad.

But it’s what the voters say they want.

www.slowboring.com/p/the-demoni...

A defense of the unfairly maligned “corporate landlord” — there’s no reason rental housing should be all owned by small-time landlords, this isn’t like the local coffee shop that’s a treasured neighborhood resource.

www.slowboring.com/p/maybe-all-...
When I wrote down "valley" as a potential story idea, what do you think I meant?

Successful military adventures like the recent one in Venezuela beget hubris and set the stage for new fiascos as arrogant leaders forget that you can’t just count on getting lucky all the time.

www.slowboring.com/p/nobody-pla...

“Stop AOC” is a terrible mission for 2028

www.slowboring.com/p/aoc-is-not...

There are levels of NIMBYism where even detached single family homes aren’t acceptable 

www.bangordailynews.com/2026/03/05/h...

From 50,000 feet I think it's easy to post on the internet "we need to return to real state capacity like in the New Deal instead of this endless reliance on contracting and outsourcing." 

But contracting is a very convenient workaround to public sector pay compression, unions, job tenure, etc
Very good points here, though I question whether progressives are prepared to really do what it would take to reduce dependence on these kind of contractors.

Want to get out ahead of this and concede that Yglesias Thought may be totally discredited as it turns out that "don't change anything, hope Trump just fucks up spectacularly in a way that sinks Republicans" works fine as a plan.
Worry less, do more (by Matthew Yglesias). Paralyzing anguish isn’t how you help people or solve problems.
Tuesday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Why Silicon Valley hasn’t done more for most Americans (by Matthew Yglesias)
Quinceañeras and Republican tumult (by Halina Bennet)
Upscale liberals are developing class consciousness (by Matthew Yglesias)
Sunday Thread + Mailbag (by Halina Bennet)
Saturday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Could the Second Mexican Empire have endured? (by Matthew Yglesias). Plus the case for proportional representation, Obama’s foreign policy, and an S-Bahn for Greater Boston
I don’t buy your “dating recession” (by Halina Bennet). The collapse of romantic infrastructure is an engineering failure, not a Gen Z character flaw.
The demonic policy strangling the British economy  (by Matthew Yglesias)
Thursday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Maybe *all* rental housing should be owned by large institutional investors (by Matthew Yglesias)

One bad provision could sink a critical bipartisan housing bill  (by Halina Bennet). Despite sweeping support and real substance, a poorly drafted investor ban could kill the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act.
Nobody plans for a quagmire  (by Matthew Yglesias). Behind every military disaster is an honest mistake.
Tuesday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
